steps.action: The Schuhplattler is a traditional style of folk with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, grounded.
steps.text: The Schuhplattler is a traditional style of folk with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, grounded.

steps.action: In this dance, the performers stomp, clap, and s
steps.text: In this dance, the performers stomp, clap, and strike the soles of their shoes (Schuhe), thighs, and knees with their hands held flat (platt).

steps.action: The more than 150 basic Schuhplattlers, as well
steps.text: The more than 150 basic Schuhplattlers, as well as marches and acrobatic feats, are often interspersed with the basic dance in performance.

steps.action: While the Schuhplattler is still largely perform
steps.text: While the Schuhplattler is still largely performed by adults, it has become increasingly popular with youngsters, who love its colorful costumes and its bouncing, leaping, kicking, and choreographed horseplay.

steps.action: Sometimes, it was performed as a partner dance,
steps.text: Sometimes, it was performed as a partner dance, with couples doing a Ländler and then splitting up so the girls could twirl in their colorful Dirndln as the boys showed off their Platteln.

steps.action: At other times, just the boys were onstage, arra
steps.text: At other times, just the boys were onstage, arranged in a circle, square, or line, plattling wildly for the audience.

steps.action: These two approaches are sometimes distinguished
steps.text: These two approaches are sometimes distinguished as the Schuhplattlertanz and Schuhplattler proper, but the "boys' dance" is at the core of both forms and is most often described.

steps.action: The immediate precursors of today's Schuhplatter
steps.text: The immediate precursors of today's Schuhplatter were the 18th-century minuet, quadrille, and Française, but unlike these courtly and highly stylized dances, the early Plattlers of the common folk were free of rules.

steps.action: The young men improvised their leaps, stomps, an
steps.text: The young men improvised their leaps, stomps, and acrobatic figures "as it struck their fancy.

steps.action: " Acrobatics were an important part of the dance
steps.text: " Acrobatics were an important part of the dance at least by the 1820s, when boys began balancing on the shoulders of their partners and stamping their feet rhythmically on the ceiling!

steps.action: The music was generally in three-quarter time, l
steps.text: The music was generally in three-quarter time, like the Ländler, and was performed on the zither or the guitar, and by 1830s, the accordion or concertina.

steps.action: Many consider the real birth of the modern Schuh
steps.text: Many consider the real birth of the modern Schuhplattler, however, to be King Maximilian II of Bavaria's excursion through the Alps in 1858, when locals performed the dance for him, and he fell in love with it.

steps.action: begins with forming a circle.
steps.text: begins with forming a circle.

steps.action: Then, while the girl is briefly separated from h
steps.text: Then, while the girl is briefly separated from her partner and continues to follow waltz steps, the boy must perform a number of difficult movements to the beat of the music.

steps.action: He turns around on his axis, slaps his thighs an
steps.text: He turns around on his axis, slaps his thighs and legs, falls to his knees, jumps in the air and throws his hat as he lets out a joyful whoop.

steps.action: Those who master the dance are cheered with vigo
steps.text: Those who master the dance are cheered with vigorous applause.

steps.action: By the late 19th century, traditional costume cl
steps.text: By the late 19th century, traditional costume clubs (Trachtenvereine) were being established throughout Bavaria and Tyrol, and soon these groups spread to German communities in America and elsewhere.

steps.action: Kniebund Lederhosen (knickers) are worn by some
steps.text: Kniebund Lederhosen (knickers) are worn by some Schuhplatter groups, but they can be uncomfortable to dance in, especially in warm weather.

steps.action: More common are short lederhosen, which range fr
steps.text: More common are short lederhosen, which range from the knee-length version favored by traditionalist groups and Munich Oktoberfest visitors to the much shorter variety worn in South Tyrol.

steps.action: While European scouts have always worn lederhose
steps.text: While European scouts have always worn lederhosen without suspenders, costume and dance groups wear either standard narrow H-bar suspenders or the wider, heavily embroidered, fancy-dress variety.

steps.action: The newer dance groups, on the other hand, are o
steps.text: The newer dance groups, on the other hand, are often composed entirely of plattlerists.

steps.action: Like their adult counterparts, they dance not on
steps.text: Like their adult counterparts, they dance not only with precision but also with a zest and enthusiasm that can leave audiences cheering.

steps.action: It is made at a faster pace in Russian folk danc
steps.text: It is made at a faster pace in Russian folk dance.

steps.action: They may be seen today in Europe and in German-i
steps.text: They may be seen today in Europe and in German-immigrant communities around the world.
